content.text: Server-Sent Events (SSE) is an HTTP-based protocol that lets a server push a continuous stream of text messages to a client over a single long-lived connection, without the client needing to poll or open a bidirectional socket. It is the mechanism behind token-by-token LLM responses, live progress feeds, and real-time dashboards in countless web applications.
Server-Sent Events is a W3C standard (the
API) and an associated wire format (

) that allows a server to emit named, ordered events to a browser or HTTP client over an ordinary HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 response. The client makes one GET request, and the server holds the response open indefinitely, writing event frames as data becomes available. Because the underlying transport is plain HTTP, SSE works through proxies, CDNs, and load balancers that would block WebSocket upgrades.
Each event frame in the stream is a block of lines terminated by a blank line. Lines beginning with
hints the reconnect delay. Every other detail — authentication, TLS, compression, routing — is handled by ordinary HTTP infrastructure, making SSE the simplest possible push technology.

In agentic AI systems SSE appears wherever a running LLM inference needs to be visible in real time: the OpenAI Chat Completions API, the Anthropic Messages API, and virtually every other hosted model API deliver tokens via SSE when
is set. Agent frameworks relay those streams to their own HTTP endpoints so that end users see partial output immediately rather than waiting for the entire completion.

LLM inference is orders of magnitude slower than a typical API call. A complete 1000-token response at 50 tokens/second takes 20 seconds to generate. Without streaming, the user stares at a blank screen for the full 20 seconds; with streaming, meaningful text appears within the first 200 milliseconds. That 100x reduction in perceived latency is the single most impactful UX improvement available to LLM application developers, and SSE is the standard mechanism for delivering it.

Beyond perceived latency, SSE matters for agent architectures because it allows the server to expose intermediate state. A multi-step reasoning agent can emit tool call arguments as they are being constructed, stream search results before the LLM has finished digesting them, and show chain-of-thought fragments that help the user build trust in the system. Without a server-push mechanism, all of that intermediate information would be invisible.

SSE also solves the polling problem. Traditional polling — the client sending a GET request every second to check for updates — wastes bandwidth, introduces artificial latency, and hammers the server with empty responses. SSE replaces N polling requests per second with a single persistent connection and zero wasted round trips.
— works over plain HTTP/1.1; no special server configuration, no WebSocket upgrade, no protocol negotiation beyond a standard Content-Type header.
API is available in all modern browsers with no polyfill needed. Connection management, reconnection, and event parsing are handled by the browser runtime.
— when a connection drops the browser automatically reconnects and sends the
header so the server can replay only the missed events.
— because SSE is regular HTTP, it passes through reverse proxies, load balancers, Cloudflare, and corporate firewalls that often block WebSocket upgrades.

— each SSE connection is a single HTTP response with an open write buffer. Many async frameworks (FastAPI, Node.js, Go net/http) handle tens of thousands of concurrent SSE connections in a single process.
field lets the server multiplex distinct event categories (e.g.
) over a single connection, each routed to a separate listener.
— plain UTF-8 text lines make the stream easy to read in a terminal, log in observability tools, and parse in any language without a binary codec.

— SSE is server-to-client only. If the client needs to send data mid-stream (e.g., pause, cancel, or inject a tool result), it must open a separate HTTP request. WebSockets provide full-duplex communication in a single connection.

— browsers enforce a maximum of 6 concurrent HTTP/1.1 connections per origin. Opening multiple SSE streams from the same origin in different tabs exhausts that pool. HTTP/2 multiplexing eliminates the cap; serve over HTTP/2 in production.
— the wire format is UTF-8 text. Binary data (images, audio) must be base64-encoded before embedding, which increases payload size by approximately 33%.
— some misconfigured nginx or CDN layers buffer the response body before forwarding it, defeating streaming entirely. Correct headers (
— the server writes as fast as it can; if a slow client cannot keep up, write buffers grow. Application-level flow control must be implemented explicitly.
— if a user connects to server A but a new event originates on server B, a pub/sub broker (Redis Streams, Kafka) or sticky sessions are needed to route the event to the right connection.
The protocol is built on one deliberate design choice: the response body is never closed. The client issues a normal HTTP GET with
, then starts writing event frames into the body without ever calling the HTTP response close/end method. Each frame follows this grammar:

# Minimal event
data: some text payload

# Named event with id
event: delta
id: 5
data: {"token": "Hello"}

# Multi-line data field (joined with newline before dispatch)
data: first line
data: second line

# Control: request client reconnect delay (ms)
retry: 3000

# Comment (keepalive ping — no event dispatched)
: ping
The blank line (double newline) is the event terminator — it signals the client to dispatch the accumulated fields as a complete MessageEvent. Multiple
lines in a single event are joined with a newline character before dispatch, allowing multi-line payloads.
field is present, the browser stores it as the last event ID. On reconnect the browser sends it back as
, allowing the server to replay events 6, 7, 8 and avoid gaps. This built-in resumability is one of SSE's strongest reliability properties.

The theoretical throughput of an SSE connection is bounded by the network link, but in practice the bottleneck is almost always the producer (an LLM emitting 50–200 tokens/second) rather than the protocol. The overhead per event is minimal: each frame adds roughly 10–20 bytes of field labels on top of the payload.

A typical SSE deployment for an agentic AI application involves three layers: the client (browser or mobile app with an EventSource connection), the SSE server (a lightweight async HTTP server that holds open connections and writes events), and one or more backend data sources (LLM APIs, databases, message queues) that produce the events. The server acts as a relay and multiplexer between the stateful SSE connections and the stateless or queue-based backends.
— opens the GET connection, dispatches incoming events to named listeners, handles reconnect with Last-Event-ID automatically.
— sets response headers, maintains the open write handle, and serializes events from an async producer into the wire format.
— the upstream source of events: LLM API stream, database change notification, or message queue consumer.
— Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka routes events from any server instance to the specific SSE connection holding the relevant client session.

Think of SSE as a radio broadcast over HTTP. The client tunes in to a station (the GET request). The station (server) keeps broadcasting (writing events) without waiting for the listener to respond. If the listener's receiver loses signal (network drop), it automatically tries to find the station again and asks for a replay from the last song it heard (Last-Event-ID). The broadcast continues until the station decides it is done (server closes the connection) or the listener switches off (client calls

This contrasts with polling (repeatedly calling the radio station to ask any new songs?) and with WebSockets (a two-way phone call where both parties speak). SSE is the right abstraction when the communication is inherently one-directional: the server has a story to tell and the client should listen without interrupting.
— the canonical use case. The server proxies the LLM API stream to the browser, reducing apparent latency from seconds to milliseconds.
— multi-step agents emit status events (tool called, tool result received, thinking) so users see a live trace instead of a frozen spinner.
— metrics, logs, and alert feeds that update continuously without polling.
— push browser notifications for new emails, build completions, or order status changes.
— report the percentage completion of a long-running server-side task.
— chat apps, multiplayer games, and collaborative editors where the client also sends high-frequency events. Use WebSockets instead.
— audio/video streaming. Use WebSockets or WebRTC for binary payloads.
— sub-millisecond financial tick data or gaming state at 60Hz. SSE frame overhead and HTTP/1.1 limitations make WebSockets or custom UDP protocols a better choice.

Implementing SSE requires changes on both the server (emit events) and the client (consume events). The server-side pattern is identical across frameworks: set the correct headers, keep the connection open, and write event frames. The client-side pattern is equally simple: instantiate
Keep the response open; use an async generator or event queue to feed data.
) every 15-30 seconds to prevent proxy and load-balancer timeouts.
Close the connection cleanly after the final event (or on client disconnect).
Minimal Python SSE server with FastAPI

# requirements: fastapi uvicorn
from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio

app = FastAPI()

async def event_generator():
    """Yield SSE-formatted frames."""
    for i in range(1, 6):
        # Each frame: one or more field lines followed by a blank line
        yield f"id: {i}\ndata: Message {i}\n\n"
        await asyncio.sleep(1)  # simulate slow data source
    # Signal the client that the stream is finished
    yield "event: done\ndata: stream complete\n\n"

@app.get("/events")
async def stream_events():
    headers = {
        "Content-Type": "text/event-stream",
        "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
        "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable nginx buffering
        "Connection": "keep-alive",
    }
    return StreamingResponse(
        event_generator(),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers=headers,
    )

# Run: uvicorn server:app --reload
# Test: curl -N http://localhost:8000/events
The following example proxies an Anthropic streaming response to the browser as SSE events, using named event types for different stream segments.

# requirements: fastapi uvicorn anthropic
import anthropic
import json
from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env

async def llm_event_generator(prompt: str):
    """Stream tokens from the Anthropic API as SSE events."""
    with client.messages.stream(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    ) as stream:
        event_id = 0
        for text_delta in stream.text_stream:
            event_id += 1
            # Emit each token as a named 'delta' event with id for resumability
            payload = json.dumps({"token": text_delta})
            yield f"id: {event_id}\nevent: delta\ndata: {payload}\n\n"

        # Emit usage stats after the stream ends
        final = stream.get_final_message()
        stats = {
            "input_tokens": final.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": final.usage.output_tokens,
        }
        yield f"event: done\ndata: {json.dumps(stats)}\n\n"

@app.post("/chat/stream")
async def chat_stream(request: Request):
    body = await request.json()
    prompt = body.get("prompt", "")
    return StreamingResponse(
        llm_event_generator(prompt),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",
        },
    )

# Test:
# curl -sN -X POST http://localhost:8000/chat/stream \
#      -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
#      -d '{"prompt": "Name five planets."}'
A production agentic coding assistant serves hundreds of simultaneous users, each expecting token-by-token output from a large language model. The architecture works as follows:
The browser establishes an SSE connection to an edge server (Cloudflare Worker or Fastly Compute). The edge worker forwards the request upstream to the application server over HTTP/2.
The application server calls the hosted LLM API with
and immediately begins relaying each SSE event frame as it arrives, with no buffering.
events to a syntax-highlighting renderer and
events to a collapsible reasoning panel, giving the user immediate visual feedback about what the model is doing.
Each event frame carries an incrementing
field. If the user's connection drops (mobile network handover), the browser reconnects with
and the server resumes from the buffered position in a Redis stream, preventing duplicate or missing tokens.

At this scale (500 concurrent streams, each holding an open HTTP/2 connection for up to 60 seconds), the SSE server layer uses less than 100MB of RAM and virtually no CPU between events. The LLM API is the only meaningful cost center.
Forgetting the blank line between events

The event terminator is a blank line (two consecutive newlines after the last field). Omitting it means the client never dispatches the event — the data accumulates in a buffer and nothing fires until the connection is eventually closed.

# WRONG: missing blank line, event never dispatches
yield f"data: {payload}\n"

# CORRECT: double newline after the last field
yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

# ALSO CORRECT: explicit blank line
yield f"data: {payload}\n"
yield "\n"
Using JSON with unescaped newlines in the data field
JSON payloads are safe only when serialized to a single line. A JSON string containing a literal newline character would split the SSE frame prematurely. Always use
and never pretty-print JSON inside a data field.

When a browser tab closes or the user navigates away, the SSE connection is severed. If the server does not detect this (by catching a write error or checking a request context), it will continue calling the LLM, consuming tokens and holding server-side state indefinitely. Always wrap the generator in a try/except/finally or check
Opening multiple SSE connections on HTTP/1.1

consumes one of the browser's 6 HTTP/1.1 connections per origin. Opening two or three SSE streams from the same origin starves regular page requests. The fix is either to serve over HTTP/2 (which multiplexes all streams over one TCP connection) or to multiplex multiple logical streams over a single SSE connection using named event types.
Always serve over HTTPS and HTTP/2.
HTTP/2 eliminates the 6-connection-per-origin limit and provides header compression on the initial GET request.
every 15-20 seconds to prevent intermediate proxies from closing idle connections.
This enables resumable delivery — the server can replay from the correct position after a reconnect.
(milliseconds) to control the browser's reconnect delay instead of relying on the default 3000ms.
Use named event types to multiplex streams.
Instead of one connection per channel, use a single SSE connection with distinct event type names and route them to separate handlers client-side.
Validate the JWT or session token on the initial GET request. The
API does not support custom request headers, so pass credentials as a URL query parameter (over HTTPS only) or use a cookie.
Detect and handle client disconnect promptly.
Cancel the upstream LLM call as soon as the client drops to avoid wasted compute and cost.
Emit a terminal event before closing.
event lets the client distinguish server finished normally from connection dropped unexpectedly, simplifying error handling.

SSE connections are extremely cheap compared to traditional HTTP request handlers because they spend most of their time waiting for new events with no CPU activity. An async Python server (FastAPI/Uvicorn) or a Node.js server can hold tens of thousands of concurrent SSE connections per core, with memory consumption in the range of 2-10 KB per connection for the write buffer plus connection metadata.

The bottleneck is usually the event producer, not the SSE layer. An LLM generating 80 tokens/second at a payload of roughly 5 bytes per token emits approximately 400 bytes/second per connection. At 10,000 concurrent connections that is 4 MB/s of outbound bandwidth — well within the capability of a single 10 Gbps server NIC.

Horizontal scaling introduces the fan-out problem: an event originating on server A must reach all clients connected to server B. The standard solution is a shared pub/sub system. Each server subscribes to a Redis channel or Kafka topic; when a new event is published to that channel from any server, every subscriber forwards it to its locally-connected clients.

HTTP/2 multiplexing is critical at scale. Under HTTP/1.1 each SSE connection requires a dedicated TCP connection; under HTTP/2 all SSE streams from a single browser share one TCP connection, reducing OS socket overhead by orders of magnitude and eliminating the 6-connection cap.
concurrent streams each emitting events at rate
bytes/second, the total outbound bandwidth requirement is

— the simplest approach; yield strings from an async generator, wrap in StreamingResponse with
— a thin Starlette/FastAPI wrapper that handles the wire format, keepalives, and Last-Event-ID resumption automatically.
from an App Router Route Handler for first-class streaming support.
function wraps any LLM stream in SSE-compatible format, with helpers for React, Svelte, and Vue.
— available in all modern browsers and Node.js 18+ (via
that supports custom headers (for Bearer token auth), POST requests, and fine-grained reconnect control.
— a streaming SSE parser for server-to-server SSE consumption; parses the
format from a Fetch response body stream.

SSE occupies a specific niche in the real-time web communication landscape. The right technology depends on the directionality of the communication, the environment (browser vs. server-to-server), and the payload type.
Fallback for very old clients; simple low-frequency updates
API does not allow setting custom request headers, which prevents the standard
pattern. The three practical solutions are:
— HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax cookies are sent automatically by the browser on EventSource requests, making this the most secure default.
— pass the token as a URL parameter (
) over HTTPS. Tokens appear in server access logs; use short-lived tokens (under 60 seconds) issued specifically for the SSE endpoint.
— replaces EventSource with a Fetch-based implementation that supports Authorization headers and POST bodies.
SSE connections from a browser are subject to the standard CORS policy. The server must include

Each SSE connection holds a server resource for its entire lifetime (potentially minutes to hours). A malicious client could open thousands of connections to exhaust the server's file descriptor or memory limit. Mitigations include:
Per-user connection limits enforced at the application layer (e.g., max 5 concurrent SSE connections per user ID).
Connection duration timeouts: close connections after a maximum duration (e.g., 5 minutes) and let the client reconnect.
Rate limiting at the API gateway level on the initial GET request using IP or user-ID-based counters.

The built-in reconnect mechanism is robust but has limits. If the server does not persist events in a replayable store (Redis Stream, database) keyed by event ID, a reconnect after a gap will miss events even though Last-Event-ID is sent. For safety-critical streams, persist all events server-side with monotonic IDs and implement a replay endpoint that the SSE handler queries on reconnect.
SSE is plain HTTP with a persistent body — a single GET response that the server never closes, flushing event frames in a text format the browser parses natively.
The EventSource API handles reconnection automatically; assigning monotonically increasing
fields enables resumable delivery via the Last-Event-ID header.
field) let a single SSE connection multiplex multiple logical channels — delta tokens, tool calls, status updates — with each routed to a separate client-side listener.
Choose SSE over WebSockets when communication is unidirectional (server pushes, client listens). Use WebSockets when the client also sends high-frequency events.
Always serve SSE over HTTPS and HTTP/2 in production to eliminate the 6-connection-per-origin browser limit and prevent eavesdropping.
Critical operational details: emit keepalive comments every 15-30 seconds, set
to prevent proxy buffering, and detect client disconnect promptly to cancel upstream LLM inference.
SSE connections are extremely lightweight — an async server can hold tens of thousands of concurrent open connections with negligible CPU and only a few KB of RAM per connection.
At scale, pair SSE with a pub/sub broker (Redis Streams, Kafka) so events generated on any server instance reach clients connected to any other instance, enabling stateless horizontal scaling.
